1983: The first handheld mobile phone reaches the public, for the price of a used car.
1985: Early handhelds refine what the DynaTAC started, while European makers begin their own first mobile designs.
1987: Battery life and bulk improve incrementally, but 'mobile' still means bricks, not pockets.
1989: The first true flip phone folds the mouthpiece away, shrinking the brick into a jacket pocket.
1991: Digital GSM standards edge closer to launch, and handsets start shedding weight ahead of the switch.
1992: Digital GSM networks switch on across Europe, drawing in a wave of new manufacturers eager for a foothold.
1994: A stylus-driven touchscreen communicator, years ahead of its time, quietly invents the smartphone.
1995: Clamshells and candybars compete for shelf space as digital networks keep expanding across Europe and Asia.
1996: The clamshell goes mainstream, popularized by a design borrowed from science fiction.
1997: A crowded field of manufacturers — from car-parts makers to defense contractors — all try their hand at phones.
1998: Nokia's mid-range candybars settle into a shape millions of people will remember for the next decade.
1999: Color arrives at the edges of the market while thinner, lighter candybars dominate the middle.
2000: Mobile ownership crosses into the mainstream, and durability becomes as much a selling point as any spec.
2001: The first touchscreen-and-stylus phones appear, and Sony's mobile division merges with Ericsson.
2002: Camera phones begin their slow crawl toward the mainstream, still mostly a novelty attachment.
2003: Cameras and keyboards diverge the market — one side chases photos, the other chases email.
2004: BlackBerry adds calling to its email pager, and a titanium flip redefines what 'thin' means.
2005: Camera resolution and music playback become genuine selling points rather than afterthoughts.
2006: Windows Mobile business phones and glossy fashion sliders compete for very different customers.
2007: A single touchscreen slab arrives and quietly redraws the entire category's blueprint.
2008: The App Store opens, turning phones into platforms that outlive their original feature list.
2009: Full touchscreens and app stores go mainstream, while keyboard phones make their last stand.
2010: Retina displays, big AMOLED panels, and Android's first true flagships turn the phone into a screen first, a phone second.
2011: Bigger screens edge toward 'phablet' territory, and Android splinters into dozens of manufacturer flavors.
2012: Android phones chase ever-thinner bodies, while Sony reclaims full ownership of its handset business.
2013: Camera sensors balloon and fingerprint sensors arrive, hardening the two-horse software race we still see today.
2014: Screens keep growing, and a low-cost challenger brand starts undercutting the establishment on price.
2015: Refinement over reinvention — most flagships this year polish what worked rather than gamble on something new.
2016: AI assistants and in-house hardware efforts mature, while a battery scandal makes global headlines.
2017: Bezels disappear and notches arrive, as edge-to-edge screens become the new flagship standard.
2018: Notches multiply across the industry, now a near-universal shorthand for 'this year's phone'.
2019: Folding glass hinges make their first commercial appearance, years before they'd become reliable.
2020: 5G rolls out at scale, just as a global pandemic keeps most of these phones' cameras pointed at empty rooms.
2021: Chip shortages squeeze supply chains, while camera systems keep absorbing more of each phone's engineering budget.
2022: Notch cutouts shrink into interactive pill shapes, and a scrappy new British brand tries to make transparency a design language.
2023: Titanium frames and on-device AI experiments start showing up in flagship spec sheets.
2024: On-device AI becomes the year's headline feature, layered onto now-familiar glass slabs.
2025: Foldables mature into a real mainstream category, and AI-driven camera and assistant features become the default pitch.
